coredata.dc:title: Advancing Lithium Metal Batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Bin
Zhang, Ji-Guang
Xu, Wu
coredata.dc:description: Considering the limited energy density of traditional graphite-anode-based lithium (Li)-ion batteries, alternative high-capacity anodes are greatly needed for the next-generation high-energy-density battery systems. In this regard, Li metal is well known to be one of the most promising anodes due to its ultrahigh capacity (3,860 mAh g−1) and the very low standard negative electrochemical potential (−3.040 V). However, dendrite growth and high reactivity of Li metal anodes result in low cycling efficiency and severe safety concerns. The recent revival of research and development on Li metal anodes has brought new, in-depth understanding and key experimental progress toward Li metal protection and enhanced performance of Li metal batteries. In this Perspective, we concisely review recent discoveries in this field and suggest possible new research directions for further development of Li metal batteries.

lithium metal battery
progress
perspective
lithium dendrite
coulombic efficiency
electrolyte
interphase
membrane
structure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Suppressing Li Dendrite Growth by Optimization of Electrolytes
Suppressing Li Dendrite Growth via Membranes and Interfacial Engineering
Pursuing Rational Design of Anode Structure

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Assistant Secretary for Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Office of Vehicle Technologies of the US Department of Energy through the Advanced Battery Materials Research program under contract no. DE-AC02-05CH11231.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of Characteristics of the Li Metal Battery Family and Challenges Facing Li Metal Anodes
Progress in Electrolyte Engineering for Li Metal Stabilization
(A–D) Schematics of different morphologies of Li films in different electrolytes: dendrites (A), well-aligned nanocolumns (B), micro-sized nodules (C), and uniform wires (D).

(E) Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of Li dendrites deposited with an areal capacity of 1.75 mAh cm−2 on Cu foil in 1.0 M LiPF6/EC-EMC electrolyte. Reprinted from Zheng et al., 14 with permission. Copyright 2017, Nature Publishing Group.

(F) SEM image of dendrite-free Li film with a self-aligned and highly ordered structure deposited in 1.0 M LiPF6/PC electrolyte with 50 ppm H2O additive, at 0.1 mA cm−2 for 15 hr. Reprinted from Qian et al., 17 with permission. Copyright 2015, Elsevier.

(G) SEM image of dendrite-free Li deposition having a nodule-like structure with rounded edges in 4 M LiFSI/DME after 1.5 hr deposition at 1.0 mA cm−2. Reprinted from Qian et al., 16 with permission. Copyright 2015, Nature Publishing Group.

(H) SEM image of large microwires of Li formed on a Li metal anode surface after 100 cycles in a LiTFSI-LiBOB/EC-EMC electrolyte with 0.05 M LiPF6 as additive. Reprinted from Zheng et al., 14 with permission. Copyright 2017, Nature Publishing Group.
Interfacial Engineering for Suppressing Li Dendrite Growth

(A) A comparison of interfacial contact between Li and LLCZN without (left) and with ALD-Al2O3 coating (right). Insets: photos of melted Li metal on top of the LLCZN surface without and with ALD coating. Reprinted from Han et al., 46 with permission. Copyright 2016, Nature Publishing Group.

(B) Morphological change and mechanistic schematics of comparisons for bare Li metal (top) and Li metal protected by hybrid elastomer/LiPON layers (bottom) after 100 cycles at 2 mA cm−2. Reprinted from Kozen et al., 52 with permission. Copyright 2017, American Chemical Society.

(C) Schematic of Li anode evolution on the Cu substrate decorated with a layer of hollow carbon spheres as Li metal cycles proceed, and top-view (left) and cross-sectional (right) morphological observations of a carbon spheres/Li/Cu composite anode after Li deposition. 53 Reprinted from Zheng et al., 53 with permission. Copyright 2014, Nature Publishing Group.
(D) Operation mechanism of a separator coated with FNC to control the dendrite growth direction. 54 Reprinted from Liu et al., 54 with permission. Copyright 2017, Nature Publishing Group.

(E) Schematic of nanoporous Al2O3 membranes combined with LiTFSI/DOL-DME liquid electrolyte for dendrite-free Li metal battery application, and SEM images showing Al2O3 membranes with pore sizes ranging from 20 to 300 nm. Reprinted from Tu et al., 55 with permission. Copyright 2017, Wiley.
New Insights for Design of Anode Architecture

(A) Proposed mechanism of Li deposition process on 3D porous Cu foil and morphological observation of 2 mAh cm−2 of Li deposited on 3D Cu current collector with a submicron skeleton. Reprinted from Yang et al., 70 with permission. Copyright 2015, Nature Publishing Group.

(B) Synopsis of obvious difference of Li-ion flux distribution and Li plating models between planar Cu foil and Cu nanowire membrane. Reprinted from Lu et al., 71 with permission. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society.

(C) Schematic of the fabrication and morphology of Li-rGO composite film as anode created through infusion of molten Li into rGO film with uniform nanogaps. Reprinted from Lin et al., 79 with permission. Copyright 2016, Nature Publishing Group.

(D) Schematics of Li metal growth in vertically aligned nanochannels based on homogeneous Li-ion flux distribution upon cycling. Reprinted from Liu et al., 80 with permission. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy and Environment Directorate, Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, 902 Battelle Boulevard, Richland, WA 99354, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering the limited energy density of traditional graphite-anode-based lithium (Li)-ion batteries, alternative high-capacity anodes are greatly needed for the next-generation high-energy-density battery systems. In this regard, Li metal is well known to be one of the most promising anodes due to its ultrahigh capacity (3,860 mAh g−1) and the very low standard negative electrochemical potential (−3.040 V). However, dendrite growth and high reactivity of Li metal anodes result in low cycling efficiency and severe safety concerns. The recent revival of research and development on Li metal anodes has brought new, in-depth understanding and key experimental progress toward Li metal protection and enhanced performance of Li metal batteries. In this Perspective, we concisely review recent discoveries in this field and suggest possible new research directions for further development of Li metal batteries.

Lithium (Li)-ion batteries have been widely used as power sources for portable electronic devices and are emerging into transportation and grid applications, but the energy density of the state-of-the-art Li-ion batteries will reach its theoretical limit soon, and new battery designs are urgently needed to satisfy the increasing demand for high-energy-density batteries. In this regard, Li metal anode material has attracted worldwide attention because of its many merits. Although practical applications of Li metal anodes are still limited by several challenges, such as dendrite growth and low coulombic efficiency, rapid development of new materials and electrode designs in recent years has overcome many bottlenecks in this field and hastened the practical applications of high-energy-density and high-performance rechargeable Li metal batteries. In this Perspective, recent significant progress on stabilization of Li metal anodes for Li metal batteries is highlighted. We also present a perspective on future directions and possibilities to better address the existing challenges of Li metal anodes and Li metal batteries.

Recent significant progress on stabilization of lithium (Li) metal anodes for Li metal batteries is highlighted, including suppressing Li dendrite growth by optimization of electrolytes and via membranes and interfacial engineering, and pursuing rational design of anode structures. Several perspectives are proposed for future directions and possibilities of better addressing the existing challenges for Li metal anodes, including stabilizing Li metal anode, inhibiting Li dendrite growth, improving Li CE, and solving many other critical issues.

ce:section.ce:para: State-of-the-art lithium (Li)-ion batteries using graphite anodes (with a theoretical specific capacity of ∼372 mAh g−1) have almost reached their theoretical specific energy density (∼350 Wh kg−1), but they still cannot provide the high energy density required for application in long-range electric vehicles. 1–5 Among alternative anode materials, Li metal has been widely recognized as the ideal candidate because it has an ultrahigh theoretical specific capacity (3,860 mAh g−1) and very low redox potential (−3.040 V versus standard hydrogen electrode). 6,7 Over the four decades of research on Li metal anodes, scientists have continuously tried to integrate Li metal into next-generation, high-energy-density, rechargeable Li metal batteries with greatly improved stability. It is worth noting that in recent years, the rapid development of cathodes alternative to conventional intercalation cathodes used in state-of-the-art Li-ion batteries, i.e., the sulfur (S) cathode for the Li-S battery and the air-electrode for the Li-air battery, has undoubtedly hastened the arrival of rechargeable Li metal batteries. However, several remaining hurdles must be surmounted prior to the practical application of Li metal anodes in rechargeable Li metal batteries: (1) uncontrollable Li dendrite growth results in severe safety issues; (2) the thermodynamic instability of Li metal due to its high Fermi energy level can cause irreversible and continuous reactions between Li and electrolyte that generate thick solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layers on the Li metal surface, consume Li and electrolyte, and increase the internal resistance, thus shortening cycle life; and (3) large volumetric and morphological changes can occur in the Li metal anode during repeated plating and stripping processes, but the aforementioned SEI films are too frail to fully suppress such significant changes in the Li metal electrode. 8–12 In Li-S and Li-air batteries, besides the above challenges for Li metal anodes, Li metal often also experiences further attack by polysulfide anions in Li-S batteries and by superoxide radical anions in Li-air batteries. Therefore, compared with traditional intercalation-type cathodes, Li metal protection in Li-S and Li-air batteries is more complicated. However, stable operation of Li metal anodes is also critical for both Li-S and Li-air batteries, since the success of these batteries heavily relies on the utilization of Li metal with high theoretical specific capacity. 13 Several Li metal battery chemistries and the main issues of Li metal anodes in these batteries are summarized in Figure 1 . Based on the aforementioned considerations, stable operation of Li metal anodes is critical for next-generation battery technologies. In this short perspective article, we briefly review the strategies reported in the literature to stabilize Li metal anodes and propose a few new strategies for protecting the Li metal for long-term stable cycling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suppressing Li Dendrite Growth by Optimization of Electrolytes
ce:section.ce:para: Development of non-aqueous liquid electrolytes has been regarded as one of the most effective ways to establish a stable and appropriate environment for deposition and stripping of Li metal, because the morphology of the deposited metallic Li is dominated by the chemical and physical properties of the electrolytes (including solvents, lithium salts, salt concentrations, and additives). 8,14–25 In conventional liquid electrolytes with typical 1 M salt concentration, the deposited Li often grows in a random, disordered way leading to mainly needle-like, mossy, or tree-type morphologies, which can be generally called dendrites (Figures 2A and 2E). 14 When certain additives are mixed into the carbonate-based electrolytes, smooth and orderly (or dendrite-free) Li morphology can be obtained (Figures 2B and 2F). 17,26,27 For example, when 0.05 M cesium hexafluorophosphate (CsPF6) was added into an electrolyte of 1 M lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) in propylene carbonate (PC), a smooth Li film with highly compact and well-aligned nanorods or nanocolumns grew. 26,27 This is for two reasons: first, the copper (Cu) substrate surface is passivated by lithium fluoride (LiF) pre-formed in situ from the electrochemical reduction of the PF6 − anion; second, Cs+ has a self-healing electrostatic shield effect because the effective reduction potential of Cs+ at very low concentration is lower than that of Li+. Highly compact, well-aligned, and smoothly grown Li nanorods could also be formed when 50 ppm water (H2O) was added into the 1 M LiPF6/PC electrolyte. 17 In this case, HF that formed from the hydrolysis of LiPF6 was electrochemically reduced to form LiF before Li deposition, and then formed an LiF-rich SEI layer on the Cu substrate surface and evened the electrical distribution along the Cu surface. However, the addition of CsPF6 or H2O did not improve the Li coulombic efficiency (CE), although the morphology of the deposited Li was significantly changed. Recently our group found that the use of additive combinations can not only maintain the Li nanorod structure but also greatly enhance the Li CE. When 2% LiAsF6 and 2% vinylene carbonate (VC) or fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) were added into the 1 M LiPF6/PC electrolyte, smooth Li deposition with highly compact and well-aligned Li nanocolumns was obtained, and the Li CE was increased from 73.2% to 96.5%. 28 The electrochemical reduction of LiAsF6 before Li deposition formed LiF and Li x As, where LiF passivated the substrate surface and Li x As acted as nanoseeds for Li growth. Meanwhile, VC or FEC polymerized to form polymeric species that bound the inorganic compounds and adhered tightly to the Li metal surface, which protected the Li metal anode well and greatly increased the cycling stability of Li metal batteries. The use of FEC as additive in electrolytes was also proven to play a key role in protecting Li metal anode by Zhang et al. 29 and Markevich et al. 30

Another dendrite-free Li deposition morphology has a micro-sized nodular structure (Figures 2C and 2G), which formed when Li was deposited in a highly concentrated electrolyte of 4 M lithium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide (LiFSI) in 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME). 16 Such Li nodules have large particle size and small surface area; thus, the side reactions between Li metal and electrolyte components are greatly decreased and the Li CE is significantly improved (>99%), leading to enhanced cycling stability of Li metal batteries. Suppression of Li dendrite growth in highly concentrated PC electrolytes was first reported by Jeong et al. in 2008. 31 Since then, suppression of Li dendrite growth, protection of the Li metal anode, and more stable Li metal batteries have been confirmed in many other superconcentrated electrolytes, i.e., 4.9 mol kg−1 LiFSI in FSI-based ionic liquids, 22 7 M lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) in 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) and DME, 19 4.5 M LiFSI in acetonitrile (AN), 20 4 M LiFSI/DME 16 and 4 M lithium nitrate (LiNO3) in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), 23 3 M LiTFSI-DME, 32 and LiTFSI-3DMSO, 33 as well as in Li-S batteries, 19 Li-O2 batteries, 23,32,33 and Li metal batteries with conventional intercalation Li-ion cathodes. 22 This improvement with highly concentrated electrolytes can be attributed to the increased salt-solvent coordination, fewer free solvent molecules, and an ionic liquid-like bulk nature. 24 Although they have lower ionic conductivities, their unusual characteristics (i.e., high reductive/oxidative/thermal stability, low volatility, high carrier density, fast electrode reaction, etc.) are beneficial for Li metal batteries. 21

Li deposition morphology as uniform wires or fibrous structure (Figures 2D and 2H) can also lead to stable operation of Li metal anodes. The use of dual-salt electrolytes of LiTFSI and lithium bis(oxalato)borate (LiBOB) in a carbonate solvent mixture (ethylene carbonate and ethyl methyl carbonate [EC-EMC]) generates uniform fibrous Li deposits that protect the Li metal anode and lead to long-term cycling stability and fast chargeability of Li metal batteries with the conventional 4-V Li-ion cathode LiNi0.4Mn0.4Co0.2O2 (NMC). 14 Moreover, addition of 0.05 M LiPF6 as additive in the above LiTFSI-LiBOB/EC-EMC dual-salt electrolyte generates more compact wiry Li deposits with robust and conductive SEI films on the Li metal surface. Meanwhile, the trace amount of LiPF6 could initiate the polymerization of EC molecules to form polycarbonates on the Li metal surface and protect the cathode substrate, aluminum (Al), from corrosion by TFSI− anions. Therefore, the Li metal anode could be well protected by the inorganic/polymeric composite SEI layers during repeated Li deposition/stripping cycles, and the uniform Li fibers could promote high charging capability because the SEI films would have lower charge-transfer resistance. Such an electrolyte has also been used in a recent study about the effects of Li capacity utilization and charge current density on the stability of Li metal anode and the performance of Li metal batteries. 34 It is worth noting that the fibrous structure of Li morphology will increase the contact of Li metal and electrolyte and aggravates side reactions, so the first-cycle CE will be lower than the Li anode with a lower surface area. If the formed SEI layer is porous or rigid or insulating, it cannot protect the Li metal well and the continuous parasitic reactions between Li and electrolyte will lead to low Li CE and short cycle life. However, if the formed SEI layer from the initial cycles is compact, thin, flexible, and more ionic conductive, the Li metal will be well protected by such a high-quality SEI so further reactions between Li metal and electrolyte will be significantly reduced so that the CE and cycle life of the Li anode will be greatly improved, as in the case of the LiPF6-added LiTFSI-LiBOB dual-salt electrolyte. Nevertheless, it would be advantageous if the first-cycle CE is also high by reducing the surface area of the Li morphology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suppressing Li Dendrite Growth via Membranes and Interfacial Engineering
ce:section.ce:para: Since a liquid electrolyte can flow, it can easily penetrate the open, porous structures of the electrodes and have more contact with the electrode inner surfaces, possibly allowing more side reactions. In addition, the highest Li CE of the electrolytes reported so far is less than 99.8%, meaning there is still 0.2% irreversible loss of the Li source and electrolyte components in each cycle. 35 The use of solid-state electrolytes may avoid such issues and also block the penetration of Li dendrites, because solid-state electrolytes have a higher modulus than that of Li metal. Polymer-based solid-state electrolytes normally have room-temperature Li+ conductivities below 10−4 S cm−1, and their use in batteries requires higher temperatures, such as 80°C. 36–38 For instance, a triblock copolymer electrolyte of P(STFSILi)-PEO-P(STFSILi) material has been proposed by Bouchet et al. It has many excellent properties, including enhanced ionic conductivity (1.3 × 10−5 S cm−1 at 60°C), transport number (>0.85), better mechanical strength, and a larger electrochemical stability window (up to 5 V versus Li/Li+). 18 However, the mechanical strength of the polymer electrolytes at such high temperature is significantly reduced, so the penetration of Li dendrites cannot be prevented.

Inorganic solid-state electrolytes have been studied for more than 25 years. 39,40 Certain sulfur-based solid-state electrolytes have been reported to have room-temperature Li+ conductivity as high as 10−2 S cm−1, 41 similar to the liquid Li-ion electrolytes, while the oxygen-based lithium superionic conductor (LiSICON) and garnet solid-state electrolytes have Li+ conductivities of about 10−3 S cm−1 and 10−4 S cm−1, respectively. 42 Nevertheless, sulfur-based solid-state electrolytes are not stable in air and are very sensitive to moisture, and oxygen-based LiSICON-type solid-state electrolytes are not stable against Li metal. Therefore, sulfur-based solid-state electrolytes must be prepared in a strictly controlled environment, while sulfur-based and LiSICON-type solid-state electrolytes need a protection layer on their surfaces to avoid direct contact with the Li metal anode. 39 Although garnet-type solid-state electrolytes are stable with Li metal, the grain boundaries of the garnet particles actually speed up the penetration of Li dendrites through the electrolyte to the cathode side, causing short circuits. 43,44 Moreover, the high interfacial resistance of solid-state electrolytes and solid-state electrodes also impedes the practical application of solid-state electrolytes in batteries. 45

Recently a considerable breakthrough was reported in solid-state or quasi-solid-state electrolytes for Li metal batteries. 46–50 Hu's group fabricated garnet-type Li7La2.75Ca0.25Zr1.75Nb0.25O12 (LLCZN) with an Al2O3 coating by atomic layer deposition (ALD), 46 Li7La3Zr2O12 (LLZO) with a Ge coating, 47 and three-dimensional (3D), ion-conducting, network-like Li6.4La3Zr2Al0.2O12 solid-state electrolytes 48 to increase the Li+ conductivity of the solid-state electrolytes and significantly decrease interfacial resistance between the garnet and the Li metal anode. As shown in Figure 3 A, Li metal can be uniformly coated on the surface of LLCZN that has been coated with ALD-Al2O3 without interfacial void space, while the bare LLCZN has poor, limited interface contact with the Li metal. 46 Also, the ultrathin Al2O3 layer improves wetting of the garnet surface, as shown by the inset photos in Figure 3A. Goodenough and coworkers reported another solid-state electrolyte, LiZr2(PO4)3, with electrochemical stability as high as 5.5 V and low interfacial resistance to Li-ion transfer. 51 More importantly, the surface of the LiZr2(PO4)3 after cycling in the Li|LiZr2(PO4)3|Li cell for 100 hr remained very dense, and no Li dendrites were observed on the surface of the cycled Li metal. This indicates that the addition of LiZr2(PO4)3 solid electrolyte could suppress Li dendrite growth. Considering the safety issues of rechargeable Li batteries, solid-state electrolytes with enhanced ionic conductivity and excellent compatibility with Li metal hold great potential.

One of the functions of solid-state electrolytes is that they serve as a strong membrane to block Li dendrite growth and then stabilize the Li metal anode. This inspired the concept of artificial SEI membranes. In recent years, interfacial engineering has been reported to play a significant role in efficiently stabilizing Li metal anodes, because the SEI film on an Li metal anode can directly affect the Li plating/stripping processes. Functional protective layers (Li3PO4, 56 Al2O3/PVdF-HFP, 57 and Li3N 58 ), also called “artificial SEI layers,” could completely coat the Li metal surface in order to insulate contact between the Li metal and electrolytes and suppress Li dendrite growth. Recently, Kozen et al. designed a hybrid elastomer/lithium phosphorous oxy-nitride (LiPON) artificial SEI layer to establish Li metal protection by mechanical stabilization via the elastomeric layer (800 nm thick) and chemical protection via the thin LiPON layer. 52 This hybrid protection layer on Li metal suppressed dendrite formation for 100 plating/stripping cycles at 2 mA cm−2 (Figure 3B). Some other functional additives (Li2S6-P2S5, 59 LiNO3-Li2S5, 60 and NiF2
61 ) were also employed to build the robust protective SEI films on Li metal. Besides the aforementioned artificial SEI films, the widely used ALD thin-film fabrication technique has been also employed to stabilize Li metal. For example, an ALD-coated Al2O3 layer 14 nm thick can efficiently protect the Li surface from corrosion upon exposure to air, as well as slow down Li corrosion in electrolytes and polysulfides, thus significantly enhancing the cycling stability of Li-S cells using the ALD-coated Li metal anode. 62

Cui's group further developed nanoscale interfacial engineering to suppress Li dendrite growth and improve cycling efficiency. They fabricated a film of interconnected hollow carbon spheres on Cu foil to promote the formation of stable SEI on the carbon spheres and column-like Li (rather than dendrites) (Figure 3C). 53 Deposited Li could be even built up between the carbon spheres and the Cu foil due to weak binding of carbon spheres with the Cu substrate. This kind of “artificial” interface layer greatly facilitates uniformity of Li-ion flux and inhibits Li dendrite growth. Thereafter, Guo's group further developed a hybrid Li reservoir in spherical carbon granules with three-dimensional (3D) skeletons, which introduced a dendrite-free Li growth and high Li utilization (>95%). 63

Recently, another effective approach to suppress Li dendrite growth by incorporating Janus separators has been separately reported by Xie's group 54 and our own group. 64 The Janus separator is formed by coating one side of the conventional polymeric separator with a thin layer of electronically conductive materials such as functionalized nanocarbon (FNC) immobilized Li ions 54 or Cu film. 64 When assembling the Li metal cells, the electronically conductive side of the separator is placed facing the Li metal anode. During cell charging or Li deposition, Li from the FNC or Cu layer and from the Li metal simultaneously grow toward each other; thus, a dense Li metal layer is gradually formed in the interface between the separator (with the electronically conductive side) and the Li metal anode, since Li growth changes direction once the dendrites meet (Figure 3D).

In addition, non-conductive 3D membranes have also been reported to stabilize Li metal anodes. Archer's group used nanoporous Al2O3 membranes infused with liquid electrolytes of LiTFSI/DOL-DME 55 or ionic liquid-based electrolyte of 1-methyl-3-propylpiperidinium/SiO2 in 1 M LiTFSI/PC 65 to promote their exceptional ability to efficiently stabilize Li metal anodes upon cycling, which occurs by decoupling the effects of ion transport and mechanical pore-size limitations on dendrite nucleus size (Figure 3E). 55 Other materials such as the 3D porous polyimide separator, 66 glass fiber separator, 67 and polymer nanofibers 68 have similar effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pursuing Rational Design of Anode Structure
ce:section.ce:para: The flat or two-dimensional (2D) metallic Li foil as anode undergoes a large thickness change during each Li stripping or deposition process so that the structure of the Li metal anode experiences significant change, especially when Li CE is not high. Considering that Li dendrite formation originates from uneven Li-ion distribution on the electrode surface, several emerging approaches have been described that focus on the design of current collectors (i.e., Cu, 69–71 nickel, 72 carbon fibers, 73–75 and graphene 76–78 ) to suppress or reduce Li structure change during repeated stripping and deposition processes. Yang et al. prepared a 3D porous Cu current collector with submicron skeleton structure by reduction of CuOH2 fiber arrays. 70 In the case of traditional planar current collectors, during the Li deposition process, the sharp end of the small Li dendrite tip that initially forms serves as a charge center in the electric field for continuous accumulation of charges. Afterward, Li metal is deposited on those sharp ends, thus amplifying the growth of Li dendrites (Figure 4 A). In contrast, for the 3D Cu foil with a submicron skeleton, numerous protuberant tips on the submicron fibers can function not only as charge centers but also as nucleation sites. Uniform electric field and homogeneously dispersed charges on the 3D Cu skeleton could be achieved to inhibit Li dendrite formation, leading to a relatively flat Li deposition layer and better Li cycling stability. Creating a different design from the aforementioned, namely vertical array architecture of the Cu substrate for tuning Li-ion distribution, Lu et al. reported a planar copper-nanowire-network current collector to decrease Li-ion flux density and enhance homogeneity of ion flux distribution throughout the interconnected Cu network with high surface area, which suppresses Li dendrite growth better than a conventional 2D Cu current collector (Figure 4B). 71

Layered reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was synthesized by Li-assisted reduction of graphene oxide. Li metal can be infused into the matrix by capillarity once the rGO is in contact with molten Li (Figure 4C). 79 More importantly, the layered Li/rGO composite reduces volume change of the Li anode to less than 20%. Liu et al. obtained homogeneous Li-ion flux distribution throughout nanochannels in their modified stainless steel (SS) electrodes (Figure 4D), a strategy that greatly enhanced uniform Li nucleation and subsequent non-dendritic growth. 80 However, the large SS matrix results in an actual decrease of areal capacity of the Li anode. Reducing the proportion of matrix would make this design more promising for practical application.

With the rapidly growing demand for high-energy-density rechargeable batteries, Li metal as the ideal anode material has regained research prominence because of its high energy density, and also has promoted persistent efforts to quickly and completely realize the commercialization of rechargeable Li metal batteries. Despite the strategies highlighted here to stabilize Li metal anodes, inhibit Li dendrite growth, and improve Li CE, many critical issues and future research directions of high-energy-density Li metal batteries remain to be carefully considered; these are summarized below.
1.
Better understanding of the SEI film on Li metal. More information regarding the morphology, formation mechanism, components, and properties of the SEI film on Li metal anodes should be investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), including cryo-TEM/SEM, electron energy loss spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. These observations and characterization techniques, especially in in situ mode, would be used to clearly understand the actual roles of the SEI during plating and stripping of Li metal, which would be helpful for rational design of electrolyte, Li anode, and separator. A good understanding of the SEI is a critical research need for producing high-efficiency deposition of metallic Li.
2.
Improving durability of electrolytes as “battery blood.” The electrolyte is regarded as one of the most important factors in Li metal protection. Seeking more appropriate stable electrolytes for Li metal batteries is still a challenge due to the high Fermi energy level of Li metal toward electrolytes. To guarantee an extremely high Li CE (>99.9%), the Li metal anode should be prevented from contacting electrolytes to suppress gradual degradation of Li metal upon cycling. A superior and efficient “blocker” with high ionic conductivity and high chemical/electrochemical stability between Li metal and electrolyte is highly desirable. Another future research direction will be optimized coordination of salt and solvent in liquid electrolytes with extremely enhanced stability toward Li metal, and toward polysulfide anions in Li-S batteries or superoxide radical anions in Li-air batteries.
3.
Reconsidering comprehensive evaluation of Li metal stability. Currently, most evaluations of Li metal stability upon repeated deposition/dissolution processes of Li metal are still conducted in Li||Li or Li||Cu cells, rather than real Li metal batteries with intercalation-type cathode, sulfur electrode, and oxygen electrode. Thus, there is still a shortage of comprehensive, accurate, and systematic understanding of Li metal stability. Also, many of the present studies on Li metal stability and cycling performance of Li metal batteries are based on excess Li and electrolyte amounts, low Li capacity utilization, and low charge current densities, which are far from the practical requirements for Li anodes and Li metal batteries. Therefore, the stability of Li metal anodes and the cell performance of Li metal batteries should be re-evaluated using limited Li amount, lean electrolyte, high areal capacity utilization (i.e., 3–4 mAh cm−2), and relatively high charge current densities (>1 mA cm−2). Furthermore, although successful experiments on Li metal protection have been reported in miniature coin cells, evaluation in scaled-up cells (i.e., pouch cells of large size capacity), especially of the pressure effect, is needed to reflect the real conditions in practical high-energy-density Li metal batteries.
4.
Pursuing non-dendritic Li metal anodes. To ensure the safety of Li metal batteries, non-dendritic Li metal anodes must be achieved via further breakthroughs in the development of cell components including electrodes, electrolytes, separators, and even current collectors. In fact, significant suppression of Li dendrite growth could be achieved by optimizing the synergetic effects of all the necessary components in a full cell, rather than simple optimization of individual components.
5.
Suppressing change of Li metal upon cycling. Compared with only ∼10% volume change of the successfully commercialized graphite anodes, rapidly increased volume change of Li metal will cause a large change in the roughness and morphology of the Li electrode (especially at the top part of the Li electrode facing the separator) as well as the formation of “dead” Li, which may have more impacts on cell integrity, operation, and balance than the dendrite itself. That is to say, the morphological change and the “dead” Li formation are still sizable challenges and more serious problems for the safe operation of Li metal batteries, even if dendrite-free Li metal is realized. More attention should be paid to suppressing the volume change of Li metal by using efficient 3D structured scaffolds/matrices. However, scaffold materials should have a relatively low electrical conductivity to prevent direct Li deposition upon them. In addition, it is highly desirable to develop some helpful techniques and conduct appropriate experiments to quantify the formation of “dead” Li and Li dendrites so as to evaluate the negative effects of Li dendrite growth and “dead” Li formation on the fateful safety issue.
6.
Controlling Li deposition on anode current collector. According to the different demands of various Li metal battery applications, Li ions are extracted from conventional cathodes, such as lithium transition metal oxides and then deposited on the bare current collectors to form metallic Li/current-collector composite anodes. Thus, the thickness of the Li deposition on the current collector could be readily tuned to better fulfill the requirements for different energy densities of Li anodes. In addition, shaping different 2D/3D current collectors with the help of 3D printing, machining, and photolithography can bring about controllable Li deposition in pre-designed hosts.
7.
Simplifying Li metal stabilization process for practical application. The many complicated processes and high cost entailed in previously reported strategies for Li metal protection are not recommended when considering the application of Li metal batteries. Low-cost, one-step in situ methods to produce pre-fabricated protection films deposited on Li metal prior to cell cycling would be very desirable. Furthermore, the film thickness could be controlled by changing deposition time and the type of components governed by salt/solvent selection.
8.
Enabling relaxed environmental controls during Li metal manufacture and cell assembly. Further development of special techniques for protection of Li metal during its production, packaging, storage, transportation, and cell assembly is much needed. Roll-to-roll tooling for continuous protection of pristine Li metal would be very helpful. Reliable packaging with extremely high stability against Li foil should be also considered to provide protection for Li metal during storage.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Assistant Secretary for Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Office of Vehicle Technologies of the US Department of Energy through the Advanced Battery Materials Research program under contract no. DE-AC02-05CH11231.

ce:section.ce:para: W.X. conceived the original idea and supervised the writing. B.L. wrote the original draft, plotted the figures, and obtained the copyright permissions. W.X. and J.-G.Z. revised the paper.
